blocks.text: Findable
by Dan Fakkeldy
An Honest Guide to Getting a Small App Discovered
Chapter 1 - The Shelf With a Million Books

Picture a bookshelf the size of a city. Not a library. A shelf. It runs for block after block in every direction, taller than you can see, and every inch of it is packed with spines pressed shoulder to shoulder. There are around two million of them. There are no signs hanging from the ceiling telling you where to go. There is no front desk. There is no kind person in a cardigan who knows where everything lives and will walk you to it. There is just the shelf, going on and on, and you, standing somewhere in the middle of it, holding the one thing you made.
That shelf is the App Store. And the thing you made is one of those two million spines.

Here is the feeling I want you to sit with for a moment, because the whole craft we are about to learn grows out of it. Nobody is walking up to that shelf and asking for your app by name. They can't. They have never heard your name. They don't know it exists. Your name, the clever one you spent three weeks deciding on, means precisely nothing to a person who has never met it. Think about how you actually find things on a shelf that size. You don't wander the aisles hoping to bump into the right book. You ask for what you need. You say, out loud or into a little search box, "I want something that helps me focus while I read." You describe the hole in your day that you're trying to fill. You arrive asking for what it does, never for who it is.

That is the lonely truth of being a small app. You can pour a year of your life into something genuinely good, something that would change a stranger's afternoon, and that stranger can be standing right in front of the shelf, wanting exactly what you built, and walk away with someone else's app in their pocket. Not because yours was worse. Because yours was invisible. The gap between "I made something people would love" and "people who would love it can find it" is wide, and it is quiet, and a lot of good apps fall into it and are never heard from again.

The app we're going to follow through this whole book is sitting at the very edge of that gap right now. It's a real app, made by one person, and we'll keep coming back to it as our worked example, the way a cooking teacher keeps coming back to one onion to show you every cut. It's an audiobook player that doubles as a study tool. It links a recorded audiobook to its written companion, an e-book or a document, and keeps every spoken word lined up with the text on screen so you can read along while you listen, then turn the parts that mattered into flashcards you'll be quizzed on later. It was built, from the very first day of code, for people whose brains don't do well with a wall of plain text. People with ADHD. People with dyslexia. People the developer describes with the term AuDHD, which simply means autism and ADHD together. It is, right now, one spine on that city-sized shelf, and not one human being on Earth is searching for it by name. We're going to spend this book learning how the right few thousand of them find it anyway.

The name for that work is App Store Optimization. People shorten it to A-S-O, and I'll use that short form from here on, but don't let the dry, corporate sound of it fool you. App Store Optimization is the craft of shaping everything about your store listing, every word, every picture, every promise, so that the right people find your app and then choose it. That's the whole thing. Not a trick. A craft, the way bread-making is a craft. Mostly patience and attention and a willingness to do the unglamorous parts well.

The cleanest way I know to picture it is to think about a real shop on a real street. Two completely different things decide whether that shop succeeds. The first is where the shop sits. Is it on a street where the right people actually walk past? Is it findable at all, or is it tucked down an alley nobody has reason to enter? The second is the shop window. Once someone is standing in front of it, does the window make them want to come in? A perfect window on a dead-end street sells nothing, because nobody's there to see it. And a busy street does you no good if your window is a smeared sheet of newspaper that tells a passerby nothing. App Store Optimization is both of those jobs at once. It's choosing the street, and it's dressing the window. Where you sit, and how you look once someone's looking.

Now, you may be wondering how this is different from advertising, because advertising is also about getting noticed. The difference is money, and the difference is time. Advertising is renting attention. You pay, people see you, and the very moment you stop paying, you vanish. It's like keeping a sign lit on a busy corner. The sign is bright as long as the meter is running, and dark the second it isn't. App Store Optimization is the opposite shape. It's the groundwork you lay into the listing itself, the words and the pictures that live there for free, working for you at three in the morning while you sleep, working the week you're on holiday, working months after you last touched them. It's slower to take hold. It asks more patience. But it compounds, and it doesn't switch off when your wallet does. For a solo developer with no advertising budget, which is exactly the person who made our example app, this isn't the nice-to-have version of marketing. It's very nearly the whole game.

And inside that game live the two halves I want planted firmly in your mind, because every chapter after this one is really about one half or the other. There is being found, and there is being chosen. Being found is the street. It's whether you even appear when somebody goes looking, whether you turn up in the search results, whether you surface when someone is browsing a category. Being chosen is the window. It's whether the person who's now looking right at you decides to tap the button and bring you home. Two different problems. Two different sets of tools. A great app fails just as completely at either one, because being found by people who don't choose you is heartbreak, and being chosen by people who never find you is impossible. Keep those two ideas in separate hands. We'll be picking them up and putting them down for the rest of the book.

So let's talk about how people actually find apps, because if you guess wrong here you'll spend your effort in all the wrong places. There are really three doors into that giant shelf. The first door is search. Someone has a need, they type words that describe it into the little search box, and they look at what comes up. The second door is browsing and editorial. That's the home of the store, the curated lists, the featured spots, the place where Apple's own editors hand-pick an app and write it a little love letter on what they call the Today tab. The third door is everything that happens outside the store entirely. A friend says, you have to get this. A link in a group chat. A reviewer's video. Somebody, somewhere, points and the person follows the point straight to your page.

All three doors are real. But for a brand-new app from a name nobody knows, they are wildly unequal in size, and you need to be honest with yourself about that. Most people who discover a new app discover it by searching. Not by being featured by Apple's editors, which is wonderful when it happens and almost entirely outside your control. Not, at the start, by word of mouth, because you don't have any mouths yet. They find you by searching. Search is the loud door, the wide door, the one with the line out front. For our example app, which has no fans yet to recommend it and no editorial feature to its name, search isn't just the main door. It's nearly the only one with people coming through it.

I want you to understand why search is such a gift, and not just a chore. When somebody types words into that box, they are doing something almost unbelievably generous. They are telling you, in their own words, in their own language, exactly what they want, at the precise moment they want it. They're not idly scrolling. They came with intent. Someone typing "read along audiobook" or "focus tool for ADHD" has handed you a perfect description of the hole in their life and a clear signal that they're ready to fill it right now. You don't have to persuade them they have a problem. You don't have to interrupt their evening with an ad. You just have to be standing in the doorway they're already walking through. That's it. Search rewards you not for shouting the loudest but for matching the most honestly. And matching honestly is a thing a solo developer can be genuinely great at, which is why this is the indie's home turf.

Now let me give you the shape of what happens after someone searches, because there's a simple chain of events that the rest of this book quietly hangs on. People in the trade call it the funnel, and the word is doing real work, because a funnel is wide at the top and narrow at the bottom. Lots go in. Fewer come out. Picture it as four steps, each one smaller than the last.

The first step is impressions. An impression is simply your app being shown to someone. They searched, or they browsed, and your listing flickered into view in their results. They haven't tapped anything. They've just laid eyes on you, your icon, your name, maybe one screenshot, in a list with a dozen others. That's the top of the funnel, the widest part. The second step is the product page view. Out of all the people who saw you in a list, some smaller number were intrigued enough to actually tap through to your page, the place where your full pitch lives, all your pictures and your description. They've walked from the street into the doorway. The third step is the install. Out of everyone who stood in your doorway and read your pitch, some smaller number again pressed the button and actually downloaded the thing. And the fourth step, the one beginners forget but the one that matters most over time, is whether they keep using it. People call that retention. Out of everyone who installed, how many open it again next week instead of letting it gather dust and quietly deleting it. Shown to you, walked in, brought you home, stayed. Four narrowing steps.

The word that ties this together is conversion. Conversion is just the fraction of people who say yes and take the next step instead of dropping out. If a hundred people see your listing in a list and ten of them tap through to your page, your conversion from impression to page view is one in ten. If forty of every hundred who reach your page go on to install, that's your conversion from page to install. Every step has its own conversion rate, its own little yes-or-no gate, and your job in App Store Optimization is to gently widen each gate so fewer of the right people fall out along the way.

Now, I should be honest with you about something here, because it shapes how this whole craft feels in practice. You would think, with a funnel this important, that Apple would hand you a beautiful dashboard showing exactly which words people search and how often. For web pages, that kind of tool exists, and it tells you that a phrase gets looked up so many thousand times a month. In the App Store, you mostly don't get that. Apple does not lay out a clean ledger of search volumes for you to read off. So a great deal of this work is done from indirect signals, from clues, from educated guesses, and from estimates that other companies sell you, none of which come with a guarantee. That means App Store Optimization is part craft and part evidence, and never a formula you can plug numbers into and get a certain answer out of. I'd rather tell you that now than have you think you're doing it wrong later when the certainty never arrives. You're working in fog, with a lantern, getting closer over time. That's the honest texture of the thing, and the makers who accept it early are calmer for it.

Here's why even a small improvement in conversion is such enormous leverage, and this is the bit I really want you to feel in your bones, because it changes how you'll spend your time. Improving your page does not get you more people at the top of the funnel. Let me say that again, because it's the most useful sentence in this chapter. A better page does not bring you more traffic. What it does is get more out of the traffic you already have. Imagine the same river of people flowing past your listing every day, the same number, unchanged. If you nudge the fraction who tap through from one in ten to one in eight, you haven't touched the size of the river at all, and yet more boats are landing on your shore. Every single day. From the same river. That improvement multiplies across every impression you will ever get, forever, for free, while you sleep. That's leverage. It's why a careful afternoon spent rewriting a single sentence on your page can be worth more than a month of frantic effort somewhere else. You're not working harder for more people. You're being a better host to the people already arriving.

So let me set our example app honestly on its starting line, because the starting line is brutal, and pretending otherwise would do you no favors. The app is brand new. Its name is invented, a made-up word that has never been typed into a search box by anyone, because why would it have been. It was made by one person, a forty-seven-year-old mail carrier in Canada, for whom this is the first serious Apple app he's ever built, made in public, out loud, from the very first day. There is no company behind him. There's no marketing department, no advertising budget, not a dollar of it. When the facts for this book were gathered, in the middle of 2026, the app hadn't even properly launched. It was in a testing phase, available to a small circle of early users, with a public release planned for the first of August. It had zero reviews. Zero ratings. Not a single star, good or bad, because not enough people had used it yet for anyone to leave one.

Read that starting line again and let it land, because it is, almost to the letter, the honest indie's starting line. No fame. No money. No crowd. No reviews. A name nobody searches. If you are making something small and good and yours, this is very likely your starting line too. And I want to gently reframe that, because told one way it sounds like a death sentence and told the other way it's the most hopeful thing in this book. Yes, this developer cannot do the things big companies do. He can't outspend anyone. Somewhere on that same shelf is a polished, well-funded competitor with seventeen thousand reviews and a marketing budget bigger than his entire savings, and in a straight contest of money and noise he loses every time, instantly, without a fight. So he isn't going to enter that contest. He's going to win a different one. He cannot out-shout the giants, but he can out-specific them. He can own the exact words and serve the exact person that the big, broad, everyone-app was never built to care about. The giant audiobook app is for everybody, which is a polite way of saying it's for nobody in particular. Our example app is for a dyslexic adult who wants to read along while they listen and turn what they learned into flashcards. That person, typing that exact, slightly awkward, very honest phrase into the search box, is a person the giant doesn't even see. And that person is winnable. The niche isn't the consolation prize for being small. The niche is the whole strategy. Out-specific, never outspend. And it means something specific and a little frightening: every single install this app gets, at least at first, has to come from a complete stranger searching for what it does. Never for who it is. Nobody is typing the app's name. They can't. So the app has to be standing in the doorway when somebody types "study audiobook" or "read along while listening" or "reading help for dyslexia," and it has to be standing there convincingly enough that the stranger walks in. That is the entire near-term survival of this app, and it runs straight through the craft we're learning.

Which brings me to the trap, the one I most want to pull you out of before we go any further, because almost every first-time maker walks into it with their eyes open and their heart full. There's an old line, from a film, that goes "build it and they will come." It's a beautiful idea and it is, for software, a quiet lie. A great app that nobody can find helps absolutely no one. It doesn't matter how good it is. Goodness that can't be discovered is, from the outside, indistinguishable from not existing at all. The stranger who would have loved it never meets it, and so for them it might as well never have been made. Building it is necessary. Building it is most of the work and all of the soul. But building it is not sufficient, and the makers who learn that lesson early are the ones whose work gets to be used instead of just admired by a handful of friends.

And here is the exact tension this whole book is built around, sitting right inside our example app, sharp as a splinter. There are tools out there that grade your store listing, that look at all your words and pictures and hand you a number out of a hundred for how well-optimized it is. Our example app, the brand-new one, the one with no reviews and no launch, scores around ninety-eight out of a hundred on its listing. Ninety-eight. Nearly perfect, by the machine's reckoning. And it has zero reviews. Zero installs from strangers. It hasn't launched. Hold those two facts together in your hands and don't let go of them, because that contradiction is the most important thing in this chapter and maybe in this book. Great metadata, all those well-chosen words and pictures, does one thing and one thing only. It makes you eligible. It earns you the right to be found and the chance to be chosen. It puts you in the doorway. It does not, cannot, manufacture demand out of nothing. It does not make people want a thing they didn't want. It does not conjure a crowd. A ninety-eight out of a hundred listing for an app nobody has tried yet is a beautifully set table in an empty room. The table being beautiful is real and it matters. But you still have to get the guests to the door, and once they sit down, the food still has to be good, or they won't come back and they'll tell their friends not to either.

That last part is a thread we'll pull on through every chapter, so let me name it now while it's small. Everything you write into your listing, every word and screenshot and claim, is a promise. And the app has to keep it. You can stuff your listing with words that don't describe your app, you can buy yourself a pile of fake five-star reviews, you can shoot screenshots of a sleeker, faster app than the one you actually built. And all of it works, briefly, in the cheap sense that it gets a stranger through your door. But all you've really done is arrange for someone to arrive, full of hope, and then be disappointed by you in person. They'll feel the gap between the promise and the thing in about ten seconds, and they'll leave, and they'll leave a one-star review on their way out that poisons the well for the next honest stranger. Over any real stretch of time, the deceptive listing and the effective listing are not two different things you get to choose between. They're the same listing, and it's the honest one, because trust is the only ingredient in this whole recipe that compounds instead of curdling. We'll come back to that promise again and again. For now, just plant the seed: a great listing is a promise made, and the only listings worth making are the ones the app can actually keep.

So carry this forward. App Store Optimization is not a clever trick for beating a machine, and the moment you start thinking of it that way you'll start making it worse. It is the patient, honest, oddly tender work of being findable by the few thousand people, out of all the billions, who would genuinely love the specific thing you made. It is matching, not shouting. From here, we're going to walk through your listing one field at a time, treating each as both a place to be found and a promise to keep, and we'll start with the single most valuable words you will ever own in that whole store: your app's own name.
Chapter 2 - Your Name Is Doing Two Jobs
Picture two strangers meeting your app in the very same instant. They both arrive in about five seconds, and they could not be more different from each other.

The first stranger is not a person at all. It is a search engine, a piece of machinery inside the App Store that is scanning a query somebody just typed, and deciding, coldly and in a fraction of a heartbeat, whether your app even deserves to appear in the results. It does not feel anything. It reads words, weighs them, and ranks them. To this stranger, your app's name is data, a string of letters to be matched against what a human just asked for. It has no taste. It cannot be charmed. It is looking for an overlap between the words you wrote and the words a person typed, and where it finds that overlap, it considers showing you. Where it does not, you simply do not exist. There is no appeal, no second glance, no benefit of the doubt.

The second stranger is a flesh-and-blood human being. They have just seen your app's name on a screen, sitting in a list among a dozen others, and they are deciding, in that same blink, whether to care. They are not weighing keywords. They are feeling something, or failing to. They are asking a quiet, half-conscious question: is this for me? Does this sound like the thing I have been looking for, or is it just more noise? This stranger has taste, and impatience, and a thumb already hovering to scroll past you. They will never see the cold calculation that put you in front of them. They only see a name, and in a heartbeat they decide whether it is worth slowing down for.

Here is the trap, and it is the whole reason this chapter exists. Your app has exactly one name, and that single name has to satisfy both of those strangers at once. The machine wants exact, literal, searchable words, the plainer the better. The human wants something that feels right, something with a little warmth or personality, something they would not be embarrassed to recommend to a friend. The machine wants you to be obvious. The human wants you to be likeable. And you get to spend the same handful of characters trying to be both. You cannot write one name for the robot and a different name for the person. There is one box, and both of them are reading out of it.

That tension never fully goes away. Some of the most important decisions you will make about getting your app discovered come down to how you resolve it, word by word, in the smallest, most crowded, most valuable text on your entire product page. So let us slow down and look at exactly where that battle is fought, and how one real app, a tiny thing built by one person, threaded the needle.

Because the good news is that you do not have to pick a winner between the two strangers. You can serve them both, if you understand what each field is for and you refuse to waste a single character. That is the craft. It is less like writing a slogan and more like packing a bag for a trip where every ounce counts. You are not asking what you would like to bring. You are asking what each item is for, and whether anything else could do that job better. Let us learn it.

There are two text fields at the very top of your app's listing, and they are, by a wide margin, the most valuable real estate you own. The first is the title. The second is the subtitle, the line that sits directly beneath it. They look modest. They are anything but.

Apple is brutally stingy with the space here. The title gets you thirty characters. Not thirty words, thirty characters, counting every letter and every space. The subtitle gets you another thirty. That is sixty characters total, less than a single short text message, to carry the heaviest load in your entire discovery strategy. By the time you have written something like "audiobook study player," you have already spent most of your title. You feel the scarcity immediately. There is no room for a sentence, no room for a clever paragraph, barely room for a phrase. Every character has to earn its place. To put that number in your hands, sixty characters is shorter than this very sentence you are listening to right now. That is the entire envelope, the whole stage on which the two biggest jobs of your listing have to be performed. Once you really feel how small that space is, you stop asking what you want to say and start asking what you can afford to say, and that shift in the question is most of the lesson.

Why do these two short fields matter so much more than, say, the long description further down the page? For two reasons that happen to stack on top of each other, which is what makes them so powerful.

The first reason is visibility. The title and subtitle are the most-seen text you have. They show up in search results. They show up on your product page. They show up in the small previews scattered around the store. Wherever your app is mentioned, the title comes along, and very often the subtitle does too. They are the face of the thing. Most of the words you will write for your listing are words a person has to choose to go and read, by tapping through and scrolling down. The title and subtitle are different. They arrive uninvited, in every place your app appears, whether anyone asked for them or not. They are the words that get seen by people who never decided to read anything at all.

The second reason is search weight. When that machine stranger is deciding whether to show your app for a given query, it does not treat all of your words equally. The words in your title carry enormous weight. The words in your subtitle carry heavy weight too. An app store scoring tool, the kind of thing that grades a listing and hands back a number, tends to treat your name and subtitle as among the very heaviest-weighted parts of the whole listing, second only to the hidden keyword field we will get to in the next chapter. So these two short lines are doing double duty: they are the most visible thing a human reads, and they are some of the strongest signal the machine reads. That is rare. Most of the time, the thing that persuades people and the thing that ranks well are different things in different places. The picture that moves a human heart does not help the machine at all. The keyword that the machine loves means nothing to a person scrolling by. But here, in these sixty characters, the two overlap. The very same words are doing the persuading and the ranking at once. Which is exactly why the overlap is worth fighting over, and exactly why you cannot afford to fill it with fluff. A wasted word here is not wasted once. It is wasted twice, in front of the person and in front of the machine, in the most valuable space you will ever own.
So hold that picture: sixty characters, doing the two biggest jobs at once. Now let us decode how a real app actually spent them.

The app we are following throughout this book is a small, real, one-person project. It is an audiobook player that doubles as a study tool, built first and foremost for neurodivergent minds, people with ADHD, with dyslexia, and with what some folks call AuDHD, meaning autism and ADHD together, and for anyone who wants to study while they listen. Its maker is a forty-seven-year-old mail carrier in Canada, building his first serious Apple app out in the open. As I tell you this, the app has not even launched yet. It is in testing, with a public release planned for the first of August. Zero ratings. Zero reviews. A complete unknown. Which makes it the perfect specimen, because a complete unknown cannot lean on a famous name. It has no reputation to coast on, no crowd already searching for it by name, no goodwill banked from a previous version. It has to earn every scrap of attention with its words. And that is the harshest, clearest, most honest version of the problem there is. If the words do not work, nothing else gets a chance to.

So look at how its title is built. The store title, read aloud, is this: audiobook study player, and then, after a small pause, the brand name Echo. Three plain, descriptive, ordinary words first, and the invented brand name last, sitting at the end like a signature.

Now, your instinct, especially if you love the thing you built, is to lead with the name. You want to say the brand first, proudly, the way the big companies do. Resist that instinct, and here is exactly why. Nobody is searching for the brand of an app they have never heard of. Think about it from the machine stranger's side. When someone opens the App Store and types, they type what they want, not what you named it. They type "audiobook." They type "study." They type "player." Those are the doors people actually walk through. They do not type the name of an app that, as far as the world is concerned, does not yet exist. They cannot search for a secret. And on launch day, your brand name is a secret, known only to you and a handful of friends.

So a no-name app spends its precious title characters on the words real people type, not on a brand nobody is searching for yet. Each of those three descriptive words is a door. "Audiobook" is a door. "Study" is a door. "Player" is a door. Put them in your title and you have built three entrances a stranger can wander through to find you. Spend that same space on your unknown brand name instead, and you have built a beautiful front door in the middle of a field where no path leads. Lovely to look at. Nobody arrives. You could carve the most gorgeous door in the world, polish the handle, hang a lantern, and it would not matter, because there is no road to it and no one knows it is there. Doors are only worth building where people are already walking. The descriptive words are exactly where people are already walking.

That is the third big idea of this whole book, and it shows up right here in the title: the honest indie's edge is being specific. You cannot out-shout the giants. You cannot outspend them on advertising, you cannot out-staff them, you cannot win a contest of who can be the loudest. But you can claim the exact plain words that describe what you actually are, and let those words carry strangers to your door. The giants are stretched wide, trying to be a little bit for everyone. You get to be exactly, unmistakably, for someone. And specific is a thing money cannot buy its way around.
Now drop your eyes one line, to the subtitle, because this is where it gets even better, and where most beginners throw away their best opportunity.

The real subtitle of our example app reads: flashcards, ADHD and dyslexia. Stop and notice what is not there. There is no slogan. There is no poetry. There is no "study smarter, not harder," no "your books, reimagined," none of the warm, empty, tagline-shaped language that beginners reach for because it feels like what a subtitle is supposed to be. Instead, every single word in that line is pulling its weight, and most of them are pulling two jobs at once.

"Flashcards" is a search term. People genuinely type that word when they are hunting for a study app, so it is a door, exactly like the words in the title, except now it is reaching a slightly different searcher, the one thinking about studying rather than the one thinking about listening. Good. That word is findable. And notice the quiet cleverness of putting it in the subtitle rather than the title. The title already caught the listener, the person whose head was full of audiobooks. The subtitle reaches sideways to a different mind entirely, the person whose head was full of studying. One small app, two kinds of searcher, caught with two different lines. That is sixty characters covering far more ground than sixty characters has any right to.

But then look at the rest of the line: ADHD and dyslexia. Those words are doing something subtler and, honestly, something beautiful. Partly they are search terms too, because there really are people who go to the App Store and type "ADHD" or "dyslexia," looking for tools made with their brain in mind. But mostly, those words are a flare fired into the sky. They are a signal that says, to a very specific human, this one is for you. Imagine you have struggled with dyslexia your whole life, you have downloaded a dozen apps that were never quite built for the way you read, every one of them designed by someone who clearly never had to fight a wall of small grey text. And then you see those words right there in the subtitle, named out loud, unashamed. That is not a keyword anymore. That is recognition. That is the app raising its hand and saying, I see you, I was made with you in mind. You stop scrolling. You did not stop because of clever marketing. You stopped because, for once, something on the shelf seemed to know you were there.

So here is the rule, and please write it on the inside of your skull. A subtitle is not a tagline. It is not the place for a clever motto. The moment you spend that line on a slogan, you commit two crimes at once. You throw away a fistful of search terms the machine would have happily indexed, and you throw away your one best chance to name your exact person out loud and make them feel seen. You lose the machine and the human in a single stroke, all for the sake of sounding poetic. Do not do it. A slogan flatters you, the maker, because it sounds like the kind of thing a real product says. But it does nothing for the two strangers you are actually trying to reach. Flattery is not the job. Reach is the job.

And notice what this does for the first big idea of the book, the one we keep circling back to. You are not optimizing for an algorithm. You are optimizing for the right human. That subtitle is not trying to please the largest possible crowd. It is trying to reach the few thousand people who will genuinely love this thing, the ones for whom "flashcards" and "ADHD" and "dyslexia" are not random words but the description of their actual life. Naming them is not a marketing trick. It is matchmaking. It is the app finding its people. And here is the part that feels backward until you have watched it work: by narrowing who you speak to, you do not shrink your audience, you find it. A name that tries to whisper to everyone is heard by no one in particular. A name that says one true specific thing to one specific person gets remembered, and repeated, and passed along, by the only people who were ever going to care in the first place.

Now I owe you the honest part, the tradeoff that sits underneath all of this, because if I only showed you the upside I would be doing you a disservice. There is a real cost to the choices we just admired, and you should feel it clearly before you make it yourself.

Every app maker dreams of a single distinctive word, one evocative name that is entirely theirs. A name like that is a gift. It is memorable. It is easy to say out loud to a friend. It looks clean and confident sitting alone at the top of a product page, with nothing crowding it. When somebody loves your app, you want them to be able to lean across a table and say one word, and have the other person find you instantly. A strong one-word brand is built for exactly that moment, the moment of the recommendation, when one happy person becomes the reason a second person arrives.

But here is the cruel arithmetic of search. The machine stranger cannot surface a word that nobody knows yet. A brand-new invented name has, by definition, no search volume, because no one is typing a name they have never heard. So the very thing that makes a bare one-word brand wonderful, its uniqueness, its newness, is the thing that makes it invisible on the day you launch. You are findable only for a word that, at the start, lives entirely inside your own head. It is a name that only pays off after enough people already know it, which is a strange kind of loan, because the brand cannot earn its keep until a crowd has heard it, and the crowd cannot hear it until something else does the work of getting them through the door. A purely descriptive name has the opposite problem. "Audiobook Study Player" is findable, sure, but it is generic. It is forgettable. It sounds like a category, not a thing you would fall in love with. Nobody recommends a category to a friend. Nobody leans across the table and whispers, you should really try audiobook study player. It does not even sound like a name. It sounds like a shelf in a store.

So look again at what that little colon construction actually buys you. Descriptive words first, then the brand at the end. It is a deliberate compromise, a way to hold both truths at once. Think of it like a person introducing themselves at a conference, wearing a name badge. The big plain line tells you what they do, accountant, nurse, teacher, the thing that tells you instantly whether you have a reason to talk to them. The smaller line is their actual name, the thing you will need later if the conversation goes well and you want to find them again. Lead with what you do, so the right people stop. Sign with who you are, so they can come back. The plain words at the front get you found today, by the strangers typing "audiobook" and "study" and "flashcards," who have never heard of you and never will unless those doors exist. And the brand name at the end gets you remembered tomorrow, so that once somebody does find you and fall for the thing, they have a name to carry around and pass along. Found by the words, remembered by the name. The construction does both jobs in one line.

And I promised you honesty, so here is the price. That title is not as clean and cool as a bare one-word brand. It cannot be. A name that reads "audiobook study player, Echo" will never have the stark, confident beauty of a single word standing alone. It looks a little workmanlike, a little like it is trying. And that is the trade. You are giving up some elegance in exchange for being discoverable on the day you most need it, the very beginning, when nobody knows your name and the only thing you have is the plain words. For an app with zero reviews and no reputation, that is almost always the right trade. Elegance is a luxury you can grow into once people are actually finding you. Discoverability is the thing that gets you to that point in the first place. Put it plainly: a beautiful name that no one can find is just a beautiful secret, and a secret, however lovely, never grew an audience. So you swallow a little pride at the top of your page, and you let the plain words do the unglamorous work of being found, because being found is the only thing that earns you the right to be elegant later.
Before we move on, you need to know the rules of the field, because there are real fences here and Apple will stop you cold if you cross them.

You cannot put a competitor's brand name in your own title or subtitle. You cannot mention a price, no "now free," no dollar figures. You cannot use superlatives, the puffed-up bragging words, no "best," no "number one," none of that. And you cannot stuff the field with a desperate pile of unrelated keywords, because Apple can and will reject a name that reads like a junk drawer of search terms rather than an honest description of the app. The store wants your name to be a name, not a billboard. Respect that, and you will save yourself a rejection and a delay. And notice that these fences are not random cruelty. They all point the same direction. They are Apple quietly insisting that your name be a true and useful description of the thing, and not a trick to borrow a rival's fame, or undercut on price, or shout a boast you cannot back up. Which is the fourth big idea of this book wearing a uniform: your metadata is a promise the app has to keep. The rules just stop you from making promises that were never yours to make.

There is one more discipline that is easy to miss and costs you real reach if you ignore it. Do not repeat the same word across your title and your subtitle. Here is the reason. For the purposes of search, those two fields are read together, combined into one pool of words. Picture the machine pouring your title and your subtitle into a single bucket and stirring. It does not keep them separate, and it does not care which line a word came from. It just has a bucket of your words, and from that bucket it builds the searches you can be found for. So if you put "audiobook" in the title and then put "audiobook" in the subtitle again, the second one is wasted. You spent precious characters covering ground you already owned. It is like a treasure map where two of your six clues point to the exact same spot, so you have really only got five clues doing the work of six. Instead, every word in the subtitle should be a new word, reaching a query the title does not. The title says audiobook, study, player. The subtitle adds flashcards, ADHD, dyslexia. Six distinct words, six different kinds of searcher, no overlap, no waste. And there is a hidden bonus the machine hands you for free. Because the two fields combine, the words can recombine across the line break. The machine can pair "study" from the title with "flashcards" from the subtitle and quietly answer someone searching for both at once, a query you never literally wrote down anywhere. Keep the words distinct and you are not just covering six searches, you are seeding the overlaps between them. That is the whole game in miniature: not how many characters you have, but how much distinct ground you can cover with them.

It helps to see the contrast with the established players, because their choices look different on purpose. Look at the real names of some of the audiobook apps already on the shelf. There is one called BookPlayer, leading with its own brand, plain and proud. There is one called Prologue, an audiobook player. There is one called Bound, an audiobook player. There is a flashcard heavyweight called AnkiMobile Flashcards, leading with its established name. Notice the pattern. The apps that lead with their brand first can afford to, because their brand is already known. People search for them by name. They have earned the right to put the name up front, because the name has become a word that real people type. Somewhere along the way, enough listeners told enough friends, and the brand quietly turned into a search term in its own right, which is the moment a name stops being a secret and starts being a door. Our example app has not earned that yet, and it is honest enough to know it. So it leads with description and signs with the brand, and it waits. Maybe, a few years from now, enough people will type that brand name that it could flip the order. That is a decision for a future with reviews and recognition behind it. Today, with zero of either, description-first is simply the truthful choice. And, please hear this clearly: the lesson here is not to copy a rival's exact name. That is the one thing the rules forbid and the one thing that would make you a follower instead of a finder. The lesson is to read the pattern and understand why each maker chose as they did, then choose honestly for where your own app actually stands. The same name placed by a famous app and an unknown one are not the same decision at all, even when they look identical on the page. Position is part of the meaning. Read where you actually stand, and choose from there.

So here is what I want you to carry out of this chapter. Your app's name is two things spoken in a single breath. It is a handshake, the warm human gesture that makes a stranger want to know you. And it is a search query, the literal plain words a machine needs to find you at all. A handshake and a search query, fused into the same sixty characters. Spend every one of those characters on a real word, a word a real person would either actually type into a search box or actually remember and say out loud to a friend. If a word does neither, it does not belong in your name. That is the test, and it is mercifully simple. Hold each word up and ask it two questions. Would somebody type you? Would somebody remember you? If the answer to both is no, the word is decoration, and you cannot afford decoration in a space this small. Send it back. Make room for a word that works.

And there is a second bank of words still waiting, one the human stranger will never, ever see. It does not appear on your product page. It does not show up in any list. It is completely invisible to people. But the machine stranger reads it closely, word by word, and it can quietly carry you to searchers your title and subtitle could never reach on their own. That hidden field is where we are headed next.
Chapter 3 - The Keyword Field Nobody Sees
There is a room in your store listing that the public never enters.

You can picture the rest of your product page easily enough, because you have stood in those rooms a thousand times as a shopper. The title at the top. The little line of subtitle underneath it. The screenshots you swipe through. The description you may or may not bother to tap "more" on. Those are the public rooms. They are decorated, lit, and arranged for human eyes, and every one of them is trying to charm you.

But there is one more room, and it has no door from the hallway. No customer ever walks into it. No reviewer photographs it. It holds about a hundred characters of text, and the only thing in the entire world that ever reads it is the search engine. You write it once, you submit it, and then it vanishes from view, quietly doing its job in the dark.

This is the hidden keyword field. And here is the strange thing about it: because nobody sees it, almost nobody treats it with respect. Two failures show up again and again. The first kind of maker forgets the field exists at all, leaves it blank or half-filled, and then wonders why their app never surfaces for the searches that should obviously find it. The second kind of maker remembers it exists and immediately abuses it, cramming it with every fashionable word they can think of, treating it like a junk drawer where you stuff anything that might possibly matter. Both are leaving the easiest, cleanest win in all of App Store Optimization sitting on the floor.

Because this room, the one nobody enters, is where you get to speak directly to the search engine with no marketing in the way. No persuasion. No personality. Just the plain truth about what your app actually does, written in the words a real person might type. It is the most honest sentence you will ever write about your app, precisely because no human will ever read it.

So let us open that door and walk in. Let us look at what is on the shelves, learn the strict little rules of the room, and then read one real, working example, term by term, until you can see exactly how a maker turns a hidden hundred characters into a quiet, accurate confession of what their app is for.
Let me describe the field itself, because the mechanics matter more here than almost anywhere else in this whole subject.

You get one hidden keyword field. Just one. It holds roughly a hundred characters, and a character is a single letter or number or comma, including the spaces if you waste any on spaces. That is your entire budget. Not a hundred words. A hundred characters. You could blow the whole thing on one long sentence about your app and have nothing left, which is exactly why nobody writes sentences in here.

Inside that field, you separate your terms with commas. And here is the first rule that almost everyone gets wrong, so I am going to be annoying about it: you put no space after the comma. None. In ordinary writing, you would type a word, a comma, a space, then the next word, because that is what looks right to a human reader. But no human is reading this. The search engine does not need the space to tell your words apart; the comma already does that. So every space you add here is a character you have spent on nothing. It is a coin dropped down a drain. With only a hundred characters to your name, spending even a handful of them on invisible spaces between terms is a genuine, measurable waste. So you crowd your words right up against the commas, no breathing room, like passengers packed onto a rush-hour train. It looks ugly. It is supposed to. Ugly and efficient is the entire aesthetic of this room.
Now the second rule, and this one is even more commonly broken, because it goes against every instinct you have about how searching works. You list single words. Not phrases. Single, separate words.

Your instinct says: but people search for whole phrases. They type "audiobook study app," not just "audiobook." So surely I should put the whole phrase "audiobook study" in my keyword field to match it. And that instinct is wrong, and acting on it will quietly waste your budget. Because Apple does something clever for you behind the scenes: it automatically combines the separate words you have listed into multi-word searches. If you list the single word "audiobook" in one spot and the single word "study" in another spot, you are already eligible to show up when someone searches "audiobook study," or "study audiobook," or any other combination of your listed words. You did not have to spend a single character joining them together. Apple joins them for you, in every order, for free.

So when a maker types out a full phrase in this field, they are paying twice. They pay for "audiobook," and then they pay all over again for a second copy of "audiobook" buried inside the phrase, and they have gained nothing, because the single word was already going to be combined with everything else. Think of it like a potluck dinner. You bring one dish, and the host combines your dish with everyone else's into a full spread. You do not need to bring the entire spread yourself. You bring your one good dish, listed once, and let the kitchen do the combining. List single words, never phrases, and you stretch a hundred characters much, much further than the maker next to you who is still typing out tidy little phrases with spaces after the commas.
Now that you know the rules of the room, let me actually walk you through a real one.

The app we are following through this whole book is a small, real, solo-built thing. An audiobook player that doubles as a study tool, made for people whose brains do not always sit still, and for anyone who wants to truly absorb what they listen to rather than let it wash over them. One developer built it, a mail carrier up in Canada, in public, from the very first day of code. And in its hidden keyword field, it carries seventeen terms.
Seventeen. Hold that number; we will come back to it, because it turns out to be the one slightly anxious choice in an otherwise tidy listing.

Here is the lovely part. If you read those seventeen terms not as a random pile but grouped by the job each one is doing, the whole field reads like a compressed, honest description of the app, written in machine shorthand. It is a portrait. Let me walk you through it the way you would walk a friend through a photograph, pointing out who everyone is.

The first group is file formats. These are the kinds of files the app knows how to open, and they matter enormously, because a person who has a particular file sitting on their phone will often search for the exact format by name, hoping to find something that can finally open it. So this app lists the word for a generic e-book, which is just a book you read on a screen. It lists the PDF format, those fixed-layout document files you have emailed yourself a hundred times. It lists the EPUB format, the open standard for reflowable e-books, the kind that adjusts to your screen and font size. It lists the M4B audiobook format, which is a special audiobook file that remembers your place and holds chapter marks, a format that, frankly, only people who really care about audiobooks have ever heard of. And it lists the everyday audio format that nearly everyone has heard of, the humble file most music and spoken-word recordings come in. Five terms, and together they quietly announce: bring me your books and your audio, in whatever shape they come, and I will open them.

The second group is the study machinery. This is the part that turns a listener into a learner. Here you find the word "spaced" and the word "repetition," sitting as two separate single words, which, remember, Apple will happily recombine into the phrase a studious person actually types. Spaced repetition is the well-proven study idea that you remember something far better if you are reminded of it at slowly widening intervals, right before you would have forgotten, rather than cramming it all at once. You will also see the word "recall," because that is the thing those reminders are training: your ability to pull a fact back up from memory on demand. There is the abbreviation S-R-S, which stands for "spaced repetition system," the umbrella term serious students use for any tool built around that widening-intervals idea. And there is the name "anki," which is the well-known, much-loved flashcard program that a great many studious people already use, and crucially, already migrate away from when they want something gentler. Listing the name of the famous tool people are leaving is a quiet, smart move: it says, if you already know that world, you already know what I do.

The third group is everyday behaviors, the plain verbs of how a person uses the app day to day. The word "read," because you read along while you listen. The word "notes," because you take them. The word "focus," because the whole thing is built to help a wandering mind stay on the page. The word "loop," because you can replay a passage over and over until it sticks. The word "offline," because it all runs on your own device with nothing phoning home, which matters to people who study on a train or a delivery route with no signal. And the word "import," because the first thing you do is bring your own files in. Six small, honest verbs describing what your hands and your attention actually do.

And then the fourth group is a single word, and it is the audience word: the term AuDHD, which is the shorthand the community itself uses for having both autism and ADHD together. That one word is a flare sent up to a very specific person, saying: this was built with you in mind, not adapted for you as an afterthought.

Stand back from all seventeen and you can see what this field really is. It is not a marketing pitch. There is no adjective doing a sales job, no "best" or "powerful" or "ultimate." It is a flat, accurate inventory of what the app eats, what it does, and who it is for. A well-built keyword field is exactly that: a compressed, truthful description of your app, written for a machine that cannot be charmed and only wants to know the facts.
Now, here is where it gets genuinely instructive, because I want you to notice not just what is in this field, but what is conspicuously missing from it.

Look again. The app is an audiobook player. The word "audiobook" is not in the hidden field. The app is a study tool. The word "study" is not in the field. It makes flashcards; the word "flashcards" is not there. It was built for people with ADHD and dyslexia; neither "ADHD" nor "dyslexia" appears anywhere in this hidden room. These are the most obvious, most central words you could possibly imagine for this app, and the maker deliberately left every one of them out.
Why on earth would you leave out your most important words?

Because they are already covered, somewhere the search engine is already reading. The title and the subtitle of the app, the public lines we spent the last chapter on, already contain words like audiobook and study and the audience the app serves. And search already indexes every word in your title and subtitle. It is already reading them, already matching on them, already counting them in your favor. They are doing their job out in the lit, public part of the listing.

So if you were to also type "audiobook" into your hidden field, you would be spending precious characters to tell the search engine something it already knows from your title. You would be paying a second time for a result you already own. That is the single most common way makers waste this field: they fill it with the very words already sitting in their title and subtitle, then run out of room before they get to the words that live nowhere else.

The cleanest way to think about it is that your title, your subtitle, and your keyword field are a team, like three players covering a field. You do not station two players on the same base. The title and subtitle take the big, obvious, central words, the ones that also have to read well to a human. So the hidden keyword field is freed up to go cover all the bases the others cannot reach: the file formats, the insider terms, the specific behaviors, the niche audience word. Every term in that hidden field is there precisely because it is not already covered somewhere else. The team does not duplicate. It spreads out. And a field that refuses to repeat what the title already says is a field that has twice the reach for the same hundred characters.
So far this all sounds clean and settled, like there is one right way to fill the room. There is not. And the example app shows you the seam where the certainty runs out.

When you run a listing like this through an Optimization tool, a piece of software that scores each part of your store page and flags weaknesses, most of this app's fields came back with a perfect score, a clean hundred out of a hundred. But the keyword field did not. The keyword field scored ninety-five out of a hundred. It was the only field on the entire listing that came in under perfect. And the tool gave a reason, a small worried note attached to that lost five points: seventeen keywords, it warned, may dilute your rankings.
Dilution. That is a word worth sitting with, because it points at a real and genuinely unsettled tension at the heart of this whole craft, and there is no tidy rule that resolves it.

Here is the tension. On one hand, more keywords means more chances to match. Every term you add is another doorway a searcher might walk through to find you. Breadth feels safe. On the other hand, the weight your field can lend to any single term is not infinite. There is only so much ranking strength to go around, and the more terms you spread it across, the less each one gets. Focus feels strong.

The fertilizer analogy is the one that makes it click. Imagine you have a single bag of fertilizer, and that bag holds a fixed amount, no more. If you spread that one bag over eight plants, each plant gets a generous, heavy dose, and those eight grow tall and strong. But if you take that very same bag, the same fixed amount, and try to spread it over seventeen plants, each plant now gets a thinner scattering. Nothing is poisoned, nothing dies, but no single plant gets the deep feeding it would have had. The worry the tool is raising is exactly this: by reaching for seventeen terms, the maker may have spread the field's finite strength so thin that no single term grows tall enough to rank well, when eight better-chosen terms might each have stood up strong.

And here is the honest part, the part I will not pretend to settle for you, because the people who do this for a living have not settled it either. There is no universal right number of keywords. Eight is not magically correct. Seventeen is not obviously wrong. It depends on how competitive your terms are, how strong your app is overall, how niche your words happen to be. The maker of our example app made a bet: that breadth, casting a slightly wider net across formats and behaviors and insider terms, was worth a little dilution, because so many of those terms are ones almost nobody else is even bothering to claim. That is a defensible bet. It might also be wrong. The only way to ever find out is to ship it, watch where the app actually surfaces over the following weeks, and then adjust, dropping the dead weight and concentrating the strength.

That is the real skill here, and it is worth saying plainly. This is not a craft of recitation, where you memorize the one correct list and type it in. It is a craft of judgment under uncertainty, where you place a reasoned bet, then let reality grade your homework and you respond to the grade. The ninety-five out of a hundred is not a failure. It is the tool honestly flagging a real tradeoff, and the maker honestly choosing a side. You will be making the same kind of bet, and you should make it the same way: with a reason, and with the humility to change it.
The tool flagged one more thing, smaller but useful, and it sets up the most important idea in this chapter.

It noted that keyword order matters. Now, exactly how much it matters is one of those things the search engine never fully tells you, and people argue about it. But the safe, sensible habit is simple: lead with your strongest terms. Put the words you most want to win on, the ones most central to who you are, at the front of the field. At worst it does nothing. At best it gives your best terms a small edge. It costs you nothing to do, so you do it. That is the whole rule: front-load your strength, and stop worrying about the parts you cannot measure.
But here is the insight I most want you to carry out of this room, the one that turns a list of seventeen words into a strategy.

Look back at those terms and notice that several of them are insider words. The abbreviation S-R-S means nothing to most people on earth; only a student deep in the world of memory tools would ever type those three letters. The name of that famous flashcard app is unknown to almost everyone; only someone already living in that world would search for it. The M4B audiobook format is a piece of vocabulary that only true audiobook devotees carry around in their heads. These are not popular words. Hardly anyone types them. If you measured these terms purely by how many people search them, they would look pitiful, almost a waste of space.

And yet they may be the smartest words in the whole field. Because here is the trade you are actually making: those words have almost no traffic, but a near-perfect fit. The handful of people who do type "S-R-S" or the name of that flashcard app are not random browsers who might glance at your app and move on. They are, very precisely, your person. They have told you exactly who they are by knowing that word at all. The match is so tight that the few searches you get from it are worth far more than a flood of vague traffic from some popular, generic word.

This is the honest indie's entire game, and we will spend the next chapter learning to play it deliberately. A solo maker cannot outshout a giant competitor with seventeen thousand reviews on the broad, obvious, crowded words. You will never win "audiobook" against the whole world. But you can absolutely own "the spaced-repetition app that opens M4B files for people with AuDHD," because you may be the only one in the entire store who even thought to claim those exact words. You out-specific them. You win the precise terms and the precise person nobody else is serving, and in that narrow lane you are not the underdog. You are the only dog.
So let me hand you what to carry forward out of this hidden room.

This invisible field, the one no customer will ever read, is where you quietly tell the search engine the plain truth about what your app does. No adjectives, no salesmanship, just accurate single words crowded up against their commas, leading with your strongest terms, refusing to repeat whatever your title already covers, and reaching deliberately for the precise, low-traffic, near-perfect-fit words that summon exactly the right human. You have now seen a real field, all seventeen terms of it, and you can read it like a portrait.

But a question has been hiding under this whole chapter, and it is the one that decides everything. How do you know which words are even worth chasing? Some words look absolutely wonderful, central, obvious, irresistible, and are completely, hopelessly unwinnable, owned by giants you cannot dislodge. Others look small and dull and turn out to be wide open. Choosing the right words is not about taste. It is about reading the crowd standing around each one. So next, we learn to do exactly that: to look at a word and see how many people want it, how hard it would be to win, and whether it is a door you can actually walk through.
Chapter 4 - Words With a Crowd Around Them

Picture yourself standing at the edge of a lake on a busy morning. There's one spot everyone points to, the deep middle, where the water churns because it's thick with fish. Every other boat on the lake is already pointed at that spot. The big commercial outfits, the ones with sonar and crews and nets the size of tennis courts, are parked right on top of it. And here you are, in a little rowboat you built yourself in your garage, holding a single fishing line.

The beginner's instinct, almost everyone's instinct, is to row straight for the crowded middle. It's where the fish are. It feels obvious. When you're choosing the words people might type to find your app, that same pull shows up immediately. You look at the giant words. For an app like the one we're following, that means words like "audiobook," "books," "audio," "notes." Enormous words. Words that millions of people type every single day. Casting your line into that water feels like the smart, ambitious move, because surely, surely, with that many fish swimming past, a few of them will bite on yours.

Here is the quiet tragedy of that instinct. The most crowded pond is the worst possible place for the smallest boat. Those millions of searches aren't waiting to discover you. They're being scooped up, before they ever scroll far enough to see your name, by the apps that have been sitting in that spot for ten years with seventeen thousand reviews and a marketing budget bigger than your entire life savings. You will not rank near the top for "audiobook" by launch day. You won't rank near the top for it by next year. And while you waste your few precious words pointing at the deep middle, you've ignored the quiet coves around the edges of the lake, where there are fewer fish, yes, but every single one of them is exactly the fish you're equipped to catch, and almost no other boat has bothered to show up.

That's the whole chapter, really, said once and plainly. But it's worth slowing down, because the instinct is so strong and so wrong that you have to understand precisely why before you can resist it. A small app's discoverability is its single most fragile resource. You have a handful of words you can realistically compete for. Spend them on words you cannot win, and you've spent them on nothing. Spend them on words that bring the wrong people, and you've actually spent them on something worse than nothing, which is a thing we'll get to, because it's the heart of how this all works.

So let's learn to read the lake. Let's learn which coves are worth rowing to, how to tell them apart, and why the honest indie's best move is almost never the one that feels boldest. By the end of this chapter you'll be able to look at a word and ask three quiet questions about it, and those three questions will save you from the single most common, most expensive mistake a new app maker makes.

Let's name the three forces you're juggling, because every keyword decision you will ever make is really a balancing act between them. Get comfortable with these three words, because they're the whole game.

The first is relevance. Relevance just means: does this word actually, truly, honestly describe your app? Not "could you stretch it to fit," not "is it sort of in the neighborhood," but does a person who types this word genuinely want the thing you built? In our fishing lake, relevance is whether the fish in that spot are the kind of fish you can actually catch, clean, and serve. You could cast your line into a pond full of sharks. There are certainly a lot of them. But you didn't build a boat for sharks, and if one bites, it's going to be a disaster for both of you. Relevance is the fish being the right fish.

The second force is volume. Volume means: how many people are actually typing this word into the search bar? It's the size of the school of fish. A word with high volume gets searched constantly. A word with low volume gets searched rarely. And here's the catch that trips up every beginner: Apple does not tell you the volume. They simply don't publish it. There's no official number that says "ninety thousand people searched this last month." So everyone in this craft is working with estimates, educated guesses produced by third-party tools that watch patterns and infer. When you hear someone quote a search volume, hear it the way you'd hear a weather forecast. It's informed, it's useful, and it is absolutely not the same as standing outside and feeling the rain. Volume is roughly how many fish are in the school, as best anyone can guess from the surface.

The third force is difficulty, which you'll also hear called competition. Difficulty means: given how many strong apps already own this word, how hard would it be for you to climb up the rankings for it? This is the count of other boats already parked in that spot, and crucially, how big and how dug-in those boats are. A word can have a thousand fish and be completely useless to you if forty industrial trawlers got there first and aren't going anywhere. Difficulty is the answer to "how many other boats are already here, and can I possibly squeeze in among them."

Now hold all three in your head at once, because the art is never about maximizing one. It's about the balance. The dream word is highly relevant, decently high in volume, and low in difficulty. That word is rare, often it doesn't exist, and when it does exist you grab it and you thank your luck. The real work is the trade-offs. A wildly popular word that's brutally difficult. A perfectly relevant word almost nobody searches. You're constantly weighing the right fish against enough fish against how many boats beat you here. Three questions, asked of every word. Right fish. Enough fish. How crowded. Let's go look at four real ones.

Let me walk you through four actual words, with actual numbers measured for an app exactly like ours, in the United States, in the middle of June twenty twenty-six. I want you to hear these not as trivia but as a skill you're building, the skill of reading a difficulty readout and knowing what it's really telling you. The numbers are approximate, the way a narrator says them out loud, but they're real measurements, and the lesson lives in the shape of them.

Start with "audiobook player." This is a big, central, sensible term. About a hundred and seventy apps are ranking for it, which tells you straight away it's a busy word. Its difficulty sits around fifty out of a hundred, which is genuinely moderate, not a fortress. So on paper, you might think, that's not bad, fifty is the middle, I could fight for that. And you could. But here's where you have to look past the single number to who's actually standing in the doorway. The top of "audiobook player" is crowded with giants. This is the word where you're standing shoulder to shoulder with something like BookPlayer, an app sitting on seventeen thousand reviews. The difficulty score says "moderately hard," but the human reality says "the entry barrier is real, because the front row is occupied by names so established that for a brand-new app, displacing them isn't this year's project." Moderate difficulty, but a crowded front row.

Now move to "audio flashcards." This one is narrower. Around a hundred and thirty apps are ranking, fewer than for "audiobook player," and the difficulty score is lower too, about forty out of a hundred. Lower difficulty, fewer competitors, you'd think this is the easy win. But watch what happens when you look at the entry barrier specifically, which is a different thing from the raw difficulty score. The entry barrier here is high. Why? Because even though there aren't many apps in this race, the handful sitting at the very top are entrenched. They've been there a long time, they're deeply established, and the few seats at the top are bolted to the floor. This is the crucial distinction I want you to carry: a word can have low overall difficulty and still have a high entry barrier, because "entry barrier" is asking the sharper question. It's not "how many boats are on the lake," it's "are the boats in the best spots welded in place, or can I actually slip into a top position?" Few boats, but the good seats are taken and screwed down.

Third word: "audiobook study." Now this one surprised the person who measured it, and it's a beautiful lesson in not trusting your gut. You'd think "audiobook study" is a sleepy little phrase nobody fights over. It sounds so specific, so niche, surely it's wide open. It isn't. Around a hundred and seventy apps rank for it, and the difficulty is high, about seventy-five out of a hundred, with the entry barrier sitting at the absolute maximum. The top spots for "audiobook study" are locked down, completely. So even a phrase that feels custom-built for our example app turns out to be a hard climb, because the words "audiobook" and "study" each drag in a swarm of strong, established apps, and where those two swarms overlap, the competition is fierce and dug in. The lesson: surprisingly hard, top locked tight, and you only learned that by actually measuring instead of assuming.

And let me make "entry barrier" fully concrete before we leave these three, because it's the most useful idea in this whole chapter. Entry barrier is simply this: are the top positions for a word bolted down by established apps you realistically cannot displace yet? Not "ever." Yet. It's the difference between a parking lot with open spaces and a parking lot where every spot has a car that's been there for a decade with the engine welded shut. Difficulty tells you how busy the lot is. Entry barrier tells you whether there's any point pulling in. Always ask the second question, because a word can look beatable on the difficulty number and be utterly hopeless on the entry barrier.
Which brings us to the fourth word, and the one I want you to remember when you're tempted to be greedy. The word is "notes."

On every metric a beginner would chase, "notes" looks like a trophy. Around a hundred and eighty apps rank for it, which means it's enormous, a giant word with a giant crowd around it. The difficulty sits up in the mid-eighties out of a hundred, which is very, very high, and the entry barrier is near the maximum, meaning the top is a fortress with the drawbridge up. So already, on competition alone, "notes" is a terrible bet for a new app. You would be rowing your homemade boat directly into the churning deep middle of the lake to fish elbow-to-elbow with the industrial trawlers. You will not win "notes." Nobody small wins "notes."

But here's the part I most need you to hear, the part that's actually subtle and actually important, and it has nothing to do with the difficulty. Even if you could win a sliver of "notes," even if by some miracle a few of those searchers found their way to your app, it would hurt you. Because "notes" is barely relevant. The app we're following is an audiobook player that links a book to its narration and lets you turn what you read into study cards. It is not a notes app. When somebody types "notes," they are almost always looking for a place to jot a grocery list, paste a phone number, scribble a meeting reminder. They are not looking for an audiobook study tool. So the few who'd stumble onto our app from that word would take one look, feel a flicker of confusion, and leave. They'd bounce, as the term goes. They came for one thing, found another, and turned right around.

And now follow the consequence, because this is the mechanism that makes "notes" worse than nothing. The App Store is watching. Not in a creepy way, just in a bookkeeping way. It notices when people search a word, tap your app, and immediately leave without doing anything. Every one of those silent departures is a tiny vote, and the vote says: "this app is a bad match for this word." Enough of those votes, and the store quietly concludes you don't belong there at all, and it stops showing you, not just for "notes," but it erodes its trust in your fit. You spent one of your scarce, irreplaceable words to summon a stranger to your door, watched the stranger frown and walk away, and paid for the privilege by teaching the store you're a fraud for that word. High volume with the wrong fit isn't a smaller win than a relevant word. It's a loss. It moves you backwards.

This is the first and deepest throughline of everything in this book, and "notes" is where it bites hardest. You are not optimizing for the algorithm. You are not trying to summon the largest possible crowd. You are trying to be found by the right human, the specific person who will open your app and feel like it was built for them, because it was. A thousand wrong people who bounce are worth less than ten right people who stay, and not just emotionally, not just as a nice sentiment, but mechanically, in the cold arithmetic of how the store decides who to show. The right human, every time, over the most humans. Chase fit, not size. "Notes" is the cautionary tale you'll carry with you, the beautiful, popular, poisonous word.

So if the giant words are off the table, where does a small app actually win? This is where I get to give you the best news in the chapter, the honest indie's secret road, and it has a name. It's called the long tail.

Let me give you the two halves of it. A "head term" is one of those giant, single, central words. "Audiobook." "Notes." "Books." High traffic, sky-high difficulty, and, for a niche app, usually low relevance, because a single broad word is broad by definition and rarely matches the specific thing you made. The head is where the giants live. A "long-tail" phrase, by contrast, is longer and more specific. Instead of "audiobook," it's "audiobook study player." Instead of "notes," it's "read along audiobook," or "spaced repetition from audiobooks," that last one meaning the study technique where you review material at growing intervals so it sticks, sometimes shortened to the letters S, R, S. Long-tail phrases are searched far less often. Fewer fish in the school. That's the cost, and it's real.

But look at everything you gain for that cost. The people who type "read along audiobook" are not a vague crowd of millions who might want a hundred different things. They are a small, sharp, self-selected group who want almost precisely the one thing you built. The relevance is nearly perfect. And because the phrase is specific and the searches are fewer, the giants haven't bothered to fortify it. The competition is thin. So you take a phrase with modest volume but near-total relevance and low difficulty, and that, that exact combination, is a battle a one-person app can actually win. You won't get a flood from "audiobook study player." You'll get a trickle. But the trickle is made entirely of people who light up when they see your app, who download it, who use it, who don't bounce, who maybe even leave a review.
